summary: Paul Graham traces his shifting pursuits—programming, AI, painting, startups, and essays—and shows how each phase taught him what kinds of work felt real and lasting. He argues that working on unfashionable things for genuine reasons, following curiosity over prestige, and learning through making led to his most meaningful outcomes, from Viaweb and Y Combinator to his essays and Lisp projects. The essay is a reflective account of how a messy path can still cohere into a life of deliberate work.
key_points: Early fascination with programming and Lisp shaped his approach to building and learning.
Disillusionment with AI and academia pushed him toward making things and seeking work that lasts.
Art school and painting taught him about perception, effort, and independence, but also about institutional hollowness.
Viaweb’s hard lessons on startups and growth fed directly into YC’s model and philosophy.
Publishing essays online revealed a new medium and became a core, lasting part of his work.
money_quotes: I wanted not just to build things, but to build things that would last.
It helped to have studied art, because the main goal of an online store builder is to make users look legit, and the key to looking legit is high production values.
Another thing I didn't get at the time is that growth rate is the ultimate test of a startup.
One of the most conspicuous patterns I've noticed in my life is how well it has worked, for me at least, to work on things that weren't prestigious.
Attention is a zero sum game.
key_insight: Follow work that genuinely pulls you—even if it lacks prestige—because that’s where lasting value, learning, and meaningful outcomes tend to emerge.
